In German, the infinitive is the base form of a verb. It is the unconjugated form of the verb that you would find in the dictionary. Unlike English, where the infinitive is typically preceded by "to," German infinitive verbs are one-word forms that end in -en or -n. However, there are irregular verbs that may have a different infinitive ending.

For regular verbs, the infinitive endings are:

-en Verbs:

Examples: lesen (to read), schreiben (to write), trinken (to drink)
Most German verbs belong to this group.
-n Verbs:

Example: tun (to do), sein (to be)
A few German verbs end in -n in the infinitive.
Infinitive verbs in German serve as the base for conjugation, much like in English and other languages. Conjugation involves changing the verb form to match the subject of a sentence and may also involve changes for tense, mood, and person.
Literal: “to make/do.” English separates “do” vs “make,” German machen covers both. Example: Ich mache meine Hausaufgaben → “I do my homework.”

Literal: “to have.” English “have” is general; German haben = possession, age, or feelings. Example: Ich habe ein Auto → “I have a car.”

Literal: “to have to.” Expresses obligation or necessity. Example: Ich muss lernen → “I have to study.”
native_answer: I have to wash my hands before eating
prompt: Ich ______ mir vor dem Essen die Hände waschen
answer: Ich muss mir vor dem Essen die Hände waschen
audio_dir: Ich muss mir vor dem Essen die Hände waschen

answer: Du musst dir vor dem Essen die Hände waschen

info: Literally “to give,” but in expressions like Es gibt… = “There is/are…” English “be” is more general; German uses geben for existence in this pattern. Example: Es gibt einen Hund → “There is a dog.”

answer: es gibt ein Problem an der Tafel
audio_dir: es gibt ein Problem an der Tafel

info: Literal: “to make/do.” English separates “do” vs “make,” German machen covers both. Example: Ich mache meine Hausaufgaben → “I do my homework.”

Literal: “to bring.” English “make” in sense of bringing or causing something. Example: Ich bringe das Buch → “I make/bring the book.”
Literal: “to earn.” English “make money” uses “make,” German verdienen = earn. Example: Ich verdiene Geld → “I make/earn money.”
Literal: “to make/do.” English separates “do” vs “make,” German machen covers both. Example: Ich mache meine Hausaufgaben → “I do my homework.”
